Hey, it's John and Andre. Today we are reviewing a relationship building series. Andre, I'll have you introduce it and just sort of jump right in and then we'll just ping pong back and forth from there. Alrighty. Yeah. So we've chosen Robin Hoffman's series. It's not too long. Six emails, but they're not long emails. And Robin is, his business is eating wild things that don't kill you. You hope they don't kill you. So there's an element of danger in here. And we just thought it would be an interesting series because it's so different. It's got nothing to do with the stuff that we do. In fact, neither of us know very much about this topic at all. So we're going to be kind of going back to forwards, you know, around this topic that we both don't know. Yeah, that's probably a good point to make too, that we are approaching this not as Robin's avatar. So keep that in mind when you hear the commentary that we're, if we were inside the mind of his of his prospect, we would have a different reaction. So what we're trying to do is we're trying to, we're trying to imagine what's inside the prospect's mind. And we may get that wrong. So I think we'll probably just think out loud a little bit so that our thinking is clear in case our thinking is just misguided. And Robin ultimately knows his audience. He forgot more about his audience today than we'll probably know in our lifetimes. So keep that in mind when this is more of a, you know, how we would describe the overview here. This is just more of a two people reacting to it who are not the avatar, but who are looking at it through the lens of what should a relationship building series evoke, what should it accomplish. And on that, why don't you just take it away and we'll see where it leads. Always, always interesting to see. And this is probably a good time to just mention the different, there's no right way to do this. I think, you know, looking through the document, we had Andre had very different approaches to doing these reviews. Andre reads through them and has comments and really goes in depth. And I don't want to see them in advance. I look at them about 15 minutes beforehand and really just get, I want that first initial impression. So I tend to be all over the place. Andre tends to be much more methodical. So if nothing else, I'll be here for comic relief today. Yeah, well that's, that's only because I'm a slow thinker. So I need to get a head start. Sean can, he's, he's, he's fast in real time. So this kind of makes up for that. The other thing that I just want to mention is that I'm a big fan of having these emails be just one day apart when you first establish, you know, for the most part, it's a stranger that's just opted in. They don't know Robin other than perhaps being on his website and browsing around. But I don't know, it just feels like when that gap is is even one day, two days out, it should be closer than that. Because, you know, 24 hours is still a long time. And if somebody's subscribed at whatever the time is, most ESPs will send it again around about the same time one day later. So they'll be receiving the second email right about the same time that they opted in. I don't know, it just feels like at least these first two emails should be one day apart. But probably the entire series. Yeah, I agree. I think I'd run the whole series every day. And then it just becomes easier to explain to, I mean, it sounds ridiculous, but it's easier to say, over the next five days, I'm going to show you XYZ instead of over the next 10 days, you know, every five times over the next 10 days, it just gets really unwieldy, right? And to be able to say very clearly, hey, over the next five days, I'm going to do XYZ. That my mind understands that my simple brain, okay, cool. So I'm gonna get it, I'm gonna get a daily email. And then that I think of that 24 hour rhythm helps create a little expectation too. Like, you know, to like, let's say, for example, that we signed up today, we got his first email, tomorrow we get the second one about the same time. But I think by the third and the fourth and the fifth, we're kind of expecting that to happen. We're looking for it. I don't think that's as true every 48 hours, because it's harder to see, it's harder to see the connection happening every 48 hours. We're not, we're good at recognizing patterns. And then the first email I get after I sign up, Robin says, hey, over the next six days, I'm going to take you through the 10,000 foot view of what it takes to create the number one foraging course, according to the BBC country file. That frame is so incredibly powerful. Now, like everything that follows now oozes with that credibility, right? Like, you never have to like, he never has to say another thing again, to build credibility, because everything he says now is credible. And then I think at that point, and then he switches gears, right? And so it's, you know, hey, thanks for signing up, I really appreciate it. So the frame is credibility, but then you stop. And credibility becomes its own open loop. As a, yes, further down where he says, there's nothing more fulfilling than wandering around picking, picking a bit here, but there, my first reaction when I read that. And I might just be, you know, weird or different, but I'm, I'm scared of picking plants and dying of eating a berry or something. And Anita, my wife, she's different. She's, she was born in Bulgaria left when she was 13. So she's been brought up, you know, walking around picking plants and eating them. And, and, and this was completely normal. And she, and she hasn't died yet. So, so her, her frame of reference is quite different to mine. And when we go up, up on the rock, on the rock in Gibraltar, it's a, it's a national park. And we are walking up there. Whenever I see plant trees with, with berries, always don't touch that. Wash your hands. And I'm sure I'm being overly protective. It's, it's very common. It looks like this and there's a picture of it. Maybe that can be your starting point. I don't know. But just something that, instead of just coming out and saying, pick, pick stuff here and there. I don't know what your thoughts about that are, but you live an extra big forest. Yeah, I mean, I have an orchard, I don't have berries and stuff, but I still, I don't wander into the woods, gnawing on stuff. Just, you know, I think I'm probably closer to your perspective on this than I need is. But I mean, I think something that you just said though illustrates a much larger point that's really important, which is what is happening inside the head of the prospect who's reading this? And are we addressing those things in the right order? So what you just highlighted, which I think was, is a brilliant observation is that when we start talking, he's jumped ahead. There's nothing more fulfilling than wandering, I'm picking a bit here, a bit there. And for someone who doesn't feel comfortable wandering around, picking a bit here and a bit there, like, we've jumped ahead too far. And now they're, you know, they're, you can, you can almost feel the person who's reading it pull back a little bit like, well, wait a minute, I'm not, I'm not going to wander around the local park, you know, not on, not dandelions. I'm not quite, I'm not quite there yet, which is probably the most ridiculously simplistic thing to say. But the point being that to get this chain of, it's not really, it's not a chain of causality, but it's, to think about the, the chain of thought somebody might have as a prospect, in that we have to establish, it's almost like we're going up a set of stairs, right? So like the first, I think the first stair and foraging, again, I know nothing about this market, but I think the first stair of foraging is don't die, right? Like foraging isn't a lot of fun when the first thing you forage kills you. Right? So I would, I, you know, like I'm imagining taking somebody on a staircase of beliefs or a staircase of what they might be thinking in the order that they're thinking it. Before I get to the joy of foraging, I want to make sure to cover the fears of, you know, dying, getting sick, whatever it is. Like I want to address that for it and get it out of the way. And it doesn't, you know, it doesn't have to be three emails about how, you know, this course will teach you how to not die. You can, you can frame it so that it's, you know, it's a presupposition. You can, you know, he could say in this email, you know, his email to, I mean, so when we get down to the sections, you know, without plants or the foundation of human culture, without them, humans simply would not exist foraging as an ancient art, a slow and graceful way to feed and sustain ourselves. There's nothing more fulfilling than wandering out around, picking a bit here, a bit there. So you know, something in here, there can be a presupposition that, you know, plants or the foundation of human culture without them, humans simply would not exist foraging as an ancient art, a slow and graceful way to feed and sustain ourselves. And again, this is not wordsmithing, but then at some point you could say, and of course, all of this happens, you know, all of foraging is based on knowing what, what is okay to eat and what isn't. Like that's, that's the foundational principle of foraging, like put it in there somewhere so that then somebody can relax, and they're like, oh, okay, great, foundational principle of foraging, don't die. Everything that Robin's going to share with me is built on this foundation of me not dying. Okay, check the box and move on. And this is true in every market, every market has some number of basic questions that it's, and this is where the curse of knowledge becomes a problem. And people aren't familiar with that term. The curse of knowledge is that once you know a lot about a topic, it's very difficult to imagine what it was like not to know those things. So if you're a world-class expert in foraging like Robin is, it's very difficult to remember what it was like not to know about foraging. And so it disconnects us, all of us, this is true for all of us, it disconnects us from the emotion of the beginner's experience. And it's very easy to gloss and just sort of jump past things that are really meaningful to beginners. And there's no template to follow to externalize this. It's really you have to kind of get back to the beginning and really explore what are the basics, what are the things that could be going on in my prospect's mind. And if it seems simple, don't discount it. Like, don't die seems simple. Like, I can absolutely imagine a little of course, everyone knows that foraging is based on not dying. That makes sense. But you still have to say it. Every market has things that you still have to say. All right. Yeah, let's do want to move on to the third one or just stay with this one? Yeah, just one other thing here. Pete talks about arcane books and this history. And there's just so many opportunities here to, and I've made comments a few times now about this, is to have images. Robin must have hundreds, thousands of images of him, you know, foraging like inside of woods. And whenever he's mentioning these things, I mean, this series is six emails of just pure text. There's no images unless Robin just hasn't included the images. But it doesn't look like this has any images inside it. And I think this could almost be, I think I made a comment further down that it can be like a picture book. So there's this text and then in and around it, because you go and buy any foraging book, foraging book, at least the ones that are browsed through on Amazon when you click to look inside. They full of pictures. There's no foraging book that I've come across yet that is just text. So perhaps people who are into foraging are used to seeing images. And whenever there's mention of a plant, they want to see what the plant looks like, especially when they're making these mental associations with some things are going to kill you and some things aren't, well, what does that thing look like? So, yeah, so I just think it's a wonderful opportunity to add rich images. You know, most of them, including images of Robin within the context of these various things. And it's a great way to bond with an audience is when they can see you. And obviously, if there's this sub narrative in the PS of his backstory, I mean, then you're getting this backstory, you can see the guy and you're reading this text and you feel like you're part of the story. I think that is a magical way to bond with an audience quickly. I think there's when you were just describing that I was imagining almost like a travel log kind of idea where the photos have the like, the Polaroid kind of frame and the drop shadow behind it and they're kind of turned a little bit of an angle. So it looks like it's like a travel journal right? Like so Robin sort of, you know, because he talks about, you know, one of the things he says, I travel a fair bit and hang out with traditional cultures, those who are often on the brink of extinction. I mean, and this is the thing that's so hard to communicate that a relationship building series is the first adventure somebody has with you. That's their that's their experience. And there's that whole idea, you don't get a second chance to make a first impression. Well, this the relationship building series is this first impression. Yeah, I think there's probably a way, I mean, another way to frame this entire series is you could frame it within the context of foraging is a journey. And you could even, you could even say that for you, again, this is just thinking out loud about a different theme, but a different theme could be that foraging is a journey. And that journey starts with like the, the mundane almost the walking around and making the distinction between things that are bad and things that are good from a like, will you die perspective? Or will you get sick perspective? And then there's this richness that emerges beyond that, which is, you know, how does this, how does this thing taste and how does it make me feel? And how does that different, depending on the micro climate and all of these other things, so you get this textured nuance, right? And then eventually, for those people who are most interested, this is going to lead to a spiritual journey of sorts to this reconnection with nature has all of these additional effects. But, but we don't, I don't think we can get there in three easy steps, right? Like I think, you know, just thinking out loud from his customer journey, his prospect journey, however you want to think about it. First, people need to understand the macro, right? They need to understand like the real when I say the macro, I mean, like the gross in gross, not meaning bad gross, meaning large, right? So when we teach a movement skill in the martial arts, for example, we start with a gross motor skill. Like can, can you get your body to move into these positions, right? And at a very high level, a gross level gross motor skill, then it becomes increasingly fine motor skills and nuances. And then after a long period of time, you connect it more of an energetic level and you understand sort of that nuance and then it's finer and finer and then at some point you realize that all of the physical practice of a martial art is in service to a spiritual practice, right? That metaphor is about how you're competing, you know, you're competing with an opponent who's just like you, which you're saying love and everything is really a metaphor that you're only competing with yourself. Like there's this beautiful journey, but when somebody walks onto the floor to learn a martial art, we don't start talking to them about the spiritual journey because they're just not yet equipped to understand it. It's not meaningful, it just bounces off and they're like, here, I'm here to learn how to kick stuff. Okay, great. It's through the learning how to kick stuff that we're going to make you a better human being on the other side of it. Like that's the ultimate goal, but you don't start with better human being. You certainly can hint at it and we do that with parents, we'll often say with parents that, you know, what we do here is we make good people and we do that through the mechanism of the martial art and that there are lots of components of that about the practice of the art itself and the way that we show up here in the training area and how we interact with each other. All of those things go into it, but what we're really doing is we're making better people, making ourselves better people through the expression of this martial art. But then we get done and then we start showing them how to block and how to kick and how to strike because that's where you have to start. And I think what Robin has done here is he's jumped ahead way too far, too fast. And this is something that I think he can hint at for later. I think he can say somewhere in this journey that foraging means many things, it means different things to different people. For some, it's the ability to enjoy a walk in the woods, into appreciate nature, it's some fundamental love. For others, it's a skill that develops over time where you can distinguish between, you can appreciate the subtle nuances of where something grows and when it's in season and all those things. And eventually, when you've done this long enough, it becomes a spiritual practice of reconnecting back to some of the fundamentals with the earth that make us who we are. You can hint at that all day long. But just like this email went, I felt like it went way too deep in that image just as the audience. I don't think the audience is ready at that point. It's beautiful writing. It's just something to be saved for later, I think. Yeah. So, message five. Yeah, email five. Yeah. I thought we would on this one. Yeah, like, I mean, exactly what you wrote. I love you just, I mean, you know, you're setting me up here because you know, I love structure so much. And for me, the structure of a relationship building series has to have a beginning, middle and end. This one just doesn't have an end. It just kind of fizzles. And when there's no end, I don't, I don't, I don't know what I'm supposed to do now. I don't have that sense of closure, which, you know, I've, if you think about it from a transactional, not a financial transaction, but a transactional perspective, I've been giving my attention across six days in an exchange for that attention very much like when I watched a movie or read a book and I'm exchanging my attention. I'm really exchanging that attention for a feeling. I want to have an emotional payoff from that and that the payoff, what sets up the payoff is the expectation in the beginning. That's the, that's the deal we're making. You write a really good beginning that sets things up, creates some tension. And then you take me on a journey. And then that journey is brought to a conclusion. That's the deal we've made, the implicit deal we've made as creator and reader. And this one doesn't, it doesn't deliver on the final step of the implied promise, which I don't get the closure here. I don't get the threads coming together, the sense of like, oh, okay, I've got it. There isn't a reveal here, but there's more of a bringing things together in a way that now I understand them differently than I did. And it can be, the closure can be as simple as that. Now that you're at the end of this journey, you understand that X and Y and Z. And because of that understanding, you're better equipped to do these things or to understand these things that you came here for. And really you're drawing attention to what, how, how they're different now that they're at the end of this journey. And not only have you brought one journey to a close, you've opened up the start of the next journey, with the next several journeys. One is the one that you're orchestrating, which is that every two, you know, a couple of weeks or every week, whatever it is they're getting another newsletter for you. So there's this one journey ends, another begins. And if they'd like to do their own, you know, start their own adventure, then you're telling them they can listen to your podcast, they can look at your store, they can look at your books, and you're giving them all these choices. So that, I think that's, that's, that's an important thing for, for all of us to do really well here is to have that sense of what promise that I make the reader in the beginning. And did I deliver on that promise? And how do I make it clear to a really good writer can bring it all to a close and then the reader just feels it. But that's hard. And that's an easy one to miss. So I, I think it's better to err on the side of being a little more transparent. And whatever the implied promises were in the beginning, if you've delivered on them, draw attention at the end, bring it to a closure to how you delivered on them, how they're different as a result, what they know now that they didn't, and why that matters. Like just that to me is worthwhile. And one of the thoughts I just had here too, I just, I love seeing like a sentence, I'm like, Hey, this could be a theme for whole series. The third sentence before the end, Robin says, get out of your head and come to your senses. Perfect. That's a theme. That's not, that could drive a whole series. I could drive an entire soap opera series, I could drive an entire soap opera series, I could drive an entire story of powered promotion around that one sentence. This is, this is where, you know, theme and narrative and all these get so confusing and they're so easy to get wrong and to complicate them because they think, we think they have to be so much more difficult than they, they, they are, I mean, they don't like pick something that's like, wow, that's interesting. Like when I read that, like get out of my head and come to my senses, what the hell does that mean? Well, let me tell you, over the next five emails, we're going to really dive deep into this idea of getting out of our head and coming to our senses. And on day one, let's talk about sight. Like that's, that's how themes emerge, right? It's just something to, it's like a sailboat, right? Like this, the sentence fills the sail. And they're like, Oh, I'm off. Let's see where this leads. And maybe you do that. And it, the wind fizzles out at you. You start to try to write it and you can't, it doesn't want to write itself. Like it's, feels like a knife fight. Okay. Perfect. Like that doesn't, that doesn't have legs. But sometimes you grab that sentence and you start running with it and you realize it's, it's, it's propelling. That's like that idea of a sailboat. It's just, it's filled the sails with its energy and it is just driving you forward because it has so much energy in that one sentence. And this sentence, get out of your head, come to your senses, there's a sentence like that. Yeah. And in fact, that, that accused up my, my idea I had after reading this thing. So I'm going to, so I didn't, you know, connect to the sentence as Sean's just drawn attention to, but now it makes perfect sense. So if you think about that sentence now, I'm going to go up to, you know, the beginning of the email where I have my, my other note. And then also, if you recall the piece where he says that we're the number one voted foraging course on the BBC country file. So that's, so combine that. And then my idea is that, okay, so let me take a step back and I'll be, I'll be quick because this is, this is the thing I'm for over an hour now. So Robin's near Devin. And when I went to the UK in my last trip to visit my sister and my mom, which is near Devin, well, it's, it's in Devin. So email Robin, and he was kind enough to, to meet up for lunch. And I'd never met Robin before. And he took myself and Eta and my 77 year old mother on a foraging thing. So that was my, my first experience, Eta's first experience, my mother's first experience out in the wild foraging. And it was an amazing experience. It took five hours that flew by. I didn't have any water. I didn't have any food other than having lunch. And it just, it just flew by because we were in nature learning these new things. So I was thinking that one of the things that, that Robin queued up early on, as we walked down and we started, and we started this, this, this thing is he set this, this idea up, whereas foraging is perhaps more than anything, especially in this first time, it's about an experience interacting with the wild. So there's observation, which is, which is context, you know, where's the plant. It's, it's the feel of, of a plant, the smell of it. And any last is, is what does it taste like? Obviously, at that point, hopefully it's not a poisonous plant. So I was thinking, well, how can you turn this, this RBS into an experimental journey, an experience that kind of mimics that because some people would have never been on a foraging and others will, and they had this fond memory. So I think one of the ideas I had here was that the series could, could, could start you down this, this journey and, and, you know, Robin could, could frame it. Like, you know, I'm going to take you on a, on a two mile walk through the whatever nature reserve. And although it's, it's, it's all driven by email. I think there could be parts where he breaks, there could be a link to a, to a YouTube video that's, you know, 20 seconds long, that's, and it's unlisted. So it's, it's really personal. It doesn't exist anywhere else other than within the context of this email. So for example, the first thing that Robin showed us was he, he wondered over to this plant, he says, there we go. And he, he took it in his, in his hands, he said, break your piece off. And then you, okay, now scrunch on your finger and like all the fibers break apart. And like our fingers went all green. He says, okay, now smell it. So that whole experience, you know, took a few minutes, but he could, at that point, like explain it in text. And then, you know, click this link and watch me. And then he's suddenly you, you out there in this nature reserve. And he's like a 3D picture of his hand going in and picking this thing. And maybe it's a Hawthorne or maybe it's something else. And he scrunches in his finger, smells it. And then it's back in the email again. And through the context of this two mile walk, he's breaking in and out of text, of, of, of the text across the entire series. And, you know, and that's obviously he's, I think he should still have the PS, as we mentioned at the beginning of this call, because that'll connect them to him in a very special way. But yeah, I just think then at the end of this, of this series, someone's been, they've actually been on a, like an in field experience of having been on a foraging experience with Robin as well, even though they're not actually physically there and they're kind of actually smell the plant. But because of this combination of video, sound, and reading the text and having a mental image of this thing playing out, I think it could be a very powerful way to, to, to suck somebody. And you know, not everybody's going to click the video links that they'll be mainly just reading. And some people will and they'll just get sucked in. I think, I think, yeah, I've never done this before. You know, as I've explained it now, but I think that could be an amazing way to pull people through this narrative in a way that just represents them going on this two mile walk with them. Yeah. Well, I think as you were describing it, I was sort of my litmus test for everything. As you were describing it, I was like, damn, I want to, I want to do this, I want to read this, I want to experience this thing that you're describing. And that's, I think that'll be the final point that I make, two points that I make, and I'll hand it off to you to close things up for the day. So one of them is everybody's interesting, like to just be interesting. I mean, it's, it's so easy when you sit down to write on anything, it's so easy to think like, I don't have anything to say, like, whatever, everybody's interesting. Everybody has interesting stories. Everyone has a unique perspective on things. Everyone, you know, can, everyone can do what Andre just described, which is taking on a journey, maybe not exactly the same. Maybe you're not out in the woods, but maybe it's entirely rep, you know, it's, it's all words and it's, you know, you're reconstructing some past experience, whatever it is, everybody is interesting. And just start from that perspective that, that you are and people want to hear this stuff. They've raised their hand already. They've shown you that. And I think then, you know, the second point here is, it's exactly what you just gave a master class in Andre, which is that there are a million ways to do this. But when you, when you describe one, like Andre just described, and like, when you get excited when you're talking about it and when you're thinking like, oh, this could be really fun, or if we did this, that would be really interesting. Like that's the signal. Like that's the, that's the universe whispering in your ear. Yes, do that thing. And sometimes, yeah, it runs out of steam. You know, sometimes you'll, it's a good idea. You get into it and you're like, this wasn't as great as I thought, but most of the time, when the muse whispers and the idea feels right and you start running with it, it feels even better when you're, you're running with it. And it, like, that's the signal and just look for that, be aware of that and trust that, not to be too metaphysical, but that's, that's been a, that's, that's certainly guided my way for a long time. All right, take us home, Andre. This was an hour and a half. Well, I thought, I thought we'd be wrapped up in 45 minutes or so, which is ridiculous to think about, because never in the history of our relationship have we ever finished something fast. We just get into these things and off we go. So take us home for the last few minutes here and we'll wrap it up for the day. Yeah, I mean, I'm just, I just want to say thanks to Robin for being vulnerable enough to sending us, you know, something that you've done and it's personal to you. And, you know, it's, it's never easy getting critiques, God knows, I've, I've, it critiques by my story coach and it's, and it's painful most, most of the time. So if you do end up rewriting this, yeah, it'll be, it'll be really nice if you, if you, if you'd be willing to share it with us again, and then we can, we can maybe include a link to, to the, you know, part two, this is, this is what's, you know, Robin's done there. But yeah, thanks, thanks for sharing Robin and everyone else that sent us your stuff. We had over 20,000 or 25,000 words of emails for this, for these submissions and so yeah, it was a lot to read and it was, it was fun and, you know, we've, we picked this one because we thought it was so different and there was a lot to learn. You know, that was very different to our normal world and we hope that this is valuable to everybody and we'll make sure that in the next, you know, the next time we do the review, we'll, we'll pick somebody else, you know, Robin's not going to get picked again and keep doing the work and sending us your stuff. That's me. Thanks everybody. Thank you, Robin. We appreciate it.
